title: Spin Hamiltonian as Matrix-Free Linear Map
abstract: We present an algorithm that computes the action of a generic spin Hamiltonian on a state vector on the fly, entirely avoiding explicit matrix assembly. This is achieved through mixed-radix indexing of the full tensor-product basis, which translates local spin operations into simple integer offsets. The result is an explicit framework for evaluating single- and two-site terms across arbitrary spin lattices, including mixed-spin systems. Our construction bridges the basis-indexing logic familiar from exact diagonalization with the matrix-free state-update philosophy of address-based frameworks. By writing the indexing logic in closed form, a single uniform loop applies to every site regardless of its local Hilbert-space dimension. The method is parallelizable and memory-conserving, and can be extended to restricted basis or truncated bosonic levels.
summary: This paper introduces a highly efficient, matrix-free algorithm to compute the action of a spin Hamiltonian on a quantum state vector. By using mixed-radix indexing, it avoids the massive memory overhead of explicitly assembling the Hamiltonian matrix. This makes simulating large, complex quantum spin systems feasible for workflows requiring frequent Hamiltonian updates.
main_problem: The primary challenge is overcoming the memory limitations associated with explicitly assembling the Hamiltonian matrix for simulating large quantum many-body systems.
main_result: The authors present a generic, memory-conserving algorithm to compute the action of the Hamiltonian on a state vector entirely on the fly, avoiding explicit matrix storage.
method: The method utilizes a mixed-radix indexing scheme for the full tensor-product basis, translating local spin operations into simple integer offsets to perform the matrix-vector product.
model_or_system: The framework applies to quantum many-body systems on arbitrary spin lattices, handling local spin operators ($\hat{S}$) and allowing for mixed-spin or bosonic degrees of freedom.
key_observables: The core operation is the matrix-vector product $\hat{H}|\mathbf{x}angle$; key diagnostics include computational time and operator memory scaling.
important_parameters: The method's efficiency is measured against the system dimension $D$ and the number of local interactions ($N+|P|$).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the state vector itself can be stored (the state vector bottleneck) and that the required index arithmetic is feasible within hardware word limits.
figures_summary: The notes reference a comparison (Table I) showing the superior storage scaling of the matrix-free method versus explicit sparse assembly as system dimension grows.
paper_structure: The paper develops the mixed-radix indexing logic, details the target-driven approach for calculating matrix elements, and provides pseudocode for the matrix-free matrix-vector product ($	ext{MATVEC!}$).
why_interesting: This is highly relevant as it provides a general, scalable computational backbone for simulating complex Hamiltonians in AMO physics, especially when the Hamiltonian structure changes frequently (e.g., parameter sweeps).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
